cases.holding: Warrantless, nonconsensual entry into a SUSPECT'S OWN home to make a routine felony arrest is presumptively unreasonable; an arrest warrant implicitly authorizes entry into the suspect's dwelling when there is reason to believe the suspect is within.
cases.proposition_checked: Arrest warrant + reason to believe suspect is home authorizes entry into suspect's own residence; Fourth Amendment draws a firm line at the entrance to the house.

quotes.text: an arrest warrant founded on probable cause implicitly carries with it the limited authority to enter a dwelling in which the suspect lives when there is reason to believe the suspect is within
the Fourth Amendment has drawn a firm line at the entrance to the house. Absent exigent circumstances, that threshold may not reasonably be crossed without a warrant

cases.notes: Foundational case for the page. The 'reason to believe the suspect is within' phrasing is the source of a live circuit split on its quantum (see flags).

cases.holding: To search a THIRD PARTY'S home for the subject of an arrest warrant, police need a SEARCH warrant (absent exigency or consent); an arrest warrant alone protects the suspect, not the third-party homeowner.
cases.proposition_checked: Arrest in a third party's home requires a search warrant; an arrest warrant is inadequate to protect the Fourth Amendment interests of persons not named in it.

quotes.text: whether, under the Fourth Amendment, a law enforcement officer may legally search for the subject of an arrest warrant in the home of a third party without first obtaining a search warrant. Concluding that a search warrant must be obtained absent exigent circumstances or consent, we reverse

cases.notes: Key contrast with Payton: the protected interest is the THIRD PARTY homeowner's, not the named suspect's. Steagald opinion itself recites Payton at 602-603 (arrest warrant alone suffices for suspect's own home).

cases.holding: A suspect standing in her own doorway/threshold is in a 'public' place for Fourth Amendment purposes; she cannot defeat a lawful arrest set in motion in public by retreating inside, and hot pursuit justifies the warrantless entry that follows.
cases.proposition_checked: Threshold = public place; hot pursuit into the home after an arrest begun in public is permissible.

quotes.text: a suspect may not defeat an arrest which has been set in motion in a public place, and is therefore proper under Watson, by the expedient of escaping to a private place
The fact that the pursuit here ended almost as soon as it began did not render it any the less a "hot pursuit" sufficient to justify the warrantless entry into Santana's house

cases.notes: Threshold-as-public-place holding at 427 U.S. at 42. Read alongside Lange: Santana's categorical fleeing-suspect language is now cabined for misdemeanors.

cases.holding: The gravity of the underlying offense is a key factor in the exigency analysis; warrantless home entry for a MINOR offense should rarely be sanctioned. Nighttime warrantless home arrest for a noncriminal traffic offense (DUI, civil forfeiture) is unconstitutional.

quotes.text: an important factor to be considered when determining whether any exigency exists is the gravity of the underlying offense for which the arrest is being made
application of the exigent-circumstances exception in the context of a home entry should rarely be sanctioned when there is probable cause to believe that only a minor offense ... has been committed

cases.notes: Foundational ADD. Limits home-exigency doctrine by offense gravity.

cases.holding: The exigent-circumstances rule applies even where police 'created' the exigency, SO LONG AS the police did not create it by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment (e.g., lawfully knocking and announcing is fine).
cases.proposition_checked: Police-created-exigency doctrine narrowed to a reasonableness/lawful-conduct test.

quotes.text: The exigent circumstances rule applies when the police do not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment
a warrantless entry based on exigent circumstances is reasonable when the police did not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct violating the Fourth Amendment

cases.notes: Foundational ADD. Quotes are from the slip-opinion syllabus/Opinion-of-the-Court statement (verbatim). The slip-opinion text is in <pre> blocks with mid-sentence line breaks, so multi-word grep across lines fails; quotes confirmed by reading.

cases.holding: Pursuit of a fleeing MISDEMEANOR suspect does not categorically justify warrantless home entry; courts apply a case-by-case exigency assessment. Hot pursuit of a misdemeanant is permissible only when an actual exigency (escape, evidence destruction, harm) is present.
cases.proposition_checked: Misdemeanor hot pursuit is not a categorical exigency; case-by-case.

quotes.text: pursuit of a fleeing misdemeanor suspect does not always—that is, categorically—justify a warrantless entry into a home
case-by-case assessment of exigency when deciding whether a suspected misdemeanant's flight justifies a warrantless home entry

cases.notes: Foundational ADD + CRITICAL CORRECTION. The analyze_citations auto-resolve for '594 U.S. 295' mapped to clusters 4894054 / 4894407, but reading those IDs as opinion_id returns UNRELATED 1800s Texas land cases (cluster-ID vs opinion-ID collision). Correct Caniglia-style fix: cluster 4894407 -> sub-opinion (real opinion_id) 4698186, which is the genuine Lange v. California (2021). Verified there.

corrections: Lange v. California cluster/opinion ID: routing.md did not list Lange; analyze_citations auto-resolved 594 U.S. 295 to clusters 4894054 and 4894407. Reading either as an opinion_id returns wrong/old opinions. Correct opinion_id for the real Lange majority is 4698186 (under cluster 4894407, cite 594 U.S. 295, decided 2021-06-23). Use cluster 4894407 / opinion 4698186 going forward.
Capture is purely topical (no cites given in shorthand for Payton/Steagald/Santana beyond names); all three captured names verified correct and correctly characterized.
flags: CIRCUIT SPLIT (flag in page): Payton's 'reason to believe the suspect is within' lacks a defined quantum. Circuits split: some (e.g., 4th Cir.; also 5th, 9th in various forms) equate 'reason to believe' with PROBABLE CAUSE that the suspect resides there AND is present; others (e.g., 2d, 10th, 11th lines of authority) treat it as a LESSER standard akin to reasonable/articulable suspicion. Do not anchor the page to one circuit's rule.
Santana's categorical fleeing-suspect language is now expressly cabined by Lange for MISDEMEANANTS (case-by-case). Present Santana + Lange together.
CL cluster-ID vs opinion-ID collision affects modern (2021) SCOTUS clusters: read by sub-opinion ID, not the cluster ID, for Lange (and see Caniglia on the companion page).
